description: Explains how pytest runs test phases, turns phase results into reports, attaches captured output and logs, prints terminal progress and summaries, and manages tmp_path directories.
document:
Pytest's runtime path is organized around a small number of phases and reports. In src/_pytest/runner.py, the main runtest protocol announces that an item is starting, runs setup, call, and teardown in order, and then announces that it is finished. The setup phase prepares the collector and fixture stack, the call phase invokes the test body, and teardown unwinds state that is no longer needed for the next item. The runner also maintains the environment variable `PYTEST_CURRENT_TEST` so outside code can see the current node id and phase; it is updated for setup, call, and teardown, and removed after teardown.

`call_and_report` is the bridge between executing a phase and producing user-visible results. It chooses the appropriate runtest hook for the requested phase, wraps that hook in `CallInfo`, asks the hook system to build a test report, logs the report, and invokes interactive exception handling when an unexpected exception should be shown to debugging hooks. The call wrapper records whether the phase returned normally or raised, stores timing information, and lets process-exiting exceptions propagate when pytest should not suppress them. The collection side uses the same idea: collection is wrapped so ordinary collection failures become collection reports, while keyboard interrupts and system exits are not hidden.

Report objects in src/_pytest/reports.py are the common currency between runtime code, plugins, and terminal output. A base report knows its node id, phase, outcome, long representation, and named output sections. It can render its long representation to a terminal writer, expose a plain text version for programmatic use, and provide convenience accessors for captured stdout, stderr, and logs by reading sections with the corresponding captured-output prefixes. The same module contains serialization hooks that convert test and collection reports into built-in data structures and restore them later, adding a report type marker so callers can reconstruct the right report class.

The IO capture layers attach the content that reports later expose. The capture plugin in src/_pytest/capture.py manages global capture for collection and each runtime phase, while per-test capture fixtures take precedence when a test asks to inspect output itself. After each setup, call, or teardown block, captured stdout and stderr are added to the item as report sections. The documentation explains that pytest captures file descriptor output by default, can switch to Python stream capture or tee-style stream capture, and can disable global capture from the command line. The logging plugin follows the same report-section pattern for log records: it installs capture handlers around each phase, stores phase-specific records for the `caplog` fixture, and appends formatted log text to the item so terminal failure output can include it.

Terminal reporting in src/_pytest/terminal.py consumes those reports rather than rerunning any test logic. `TerminalReporter` keeps categorized stats, asks the status hook for each report's category and display form, and writes either compact progress letters or verbose per-test lines depending on configured verbosity and console style. At session end it prints errors, failures, warnings, selected captured sections, the short summary requested by the report-character option, and the final counts with duration. The runner contributes the slowest-duration summary by scanning reports that carry timing data and applying the configured minimum duration threshold.

Temporary directory support is another runtime IO helper. In src/_pytest/tmpdir.py, `TempPathFactory` owns the base temporary directory and creates numbered child directories for tests or session-scoped fixtures. The `tmp_path` fixture yields a unique pathlib directory based on a sanitized test name, then uses report outcomes stored during report creation to decide cleanup. With the failed-only retention policy, a path from a passing test is removed after the fixture finishes, and after a fully passing session pytest can remove the whole generated base directory when the user did not provide an explicit base temp path. When a user does provide one, pytest clears that directory at the beginning of the run instead of applying numbered-run retention.
